meta.title: DBX-DEP-2: Security, Monitoring and Storage Architecture

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to use Auto Loader to ingest JSON files that arrive continuously in an S3 bucket. They need to ensure the schema is inferred automatically and stored durably so that schema changes in new files can be detected. Which option MUST be set when configuring Auto Loader for schema inference?

options.text: `cloudFiles.inferColumnTypes`
`cloudFiles.schemaLocation`
options.explanation: `cloudFiles.schemaLocation` is required when using Auto Loader schema inference. It specifies a cloud storage path where Auto Loader persists the inferred schema across runs. Without it, Auto Loader cannot store the schema between micro-batches, so schema inference would fail or reset. When the option is set, Auto Loader compares newly inferred schemas against the stored schema to detect evolution. option **A** (`inferColumnTypes`) controls whether columns are inferred as typed or all-string, but it is not required. option **C** (`schemaHints`) lets you manually specify types for select columns but is optional. option **D** (`maxFilesPerTrigger`) controls throughput, not schema inference.

options.text: `cloudFiles.schemaHints`
`cloudFiles.maxFilesPerTrigger`

questions.stem: Which Structured Streaming trigger causes a job to process all available data in the source exactly once and then terminate the stream, behaving similarly to a batch job but retaining stream semantics like checkpointing?

options.text: `trigger(processingTime='0 seconds')`
`trigger(once=True)`
`trigger(availableNow=True)`
options.explanation: `trigger(availableNow=True)` is the modern replacement for `trigger(once=True)`. It processes all data available at the time the stream starts across multiple micro-batches (exploiting parallelism), then shuts down the stream. It retains full streaming semantics including checkpoints and exactly-once guarantees. `trigger(once=True)` also terminates after processing available data but processes everything in a single micro-batch, which can be slow for large backlogs. `processingTime='0 seconds'` runs as fast as possible but keeps the stream alive. `continuous` mode is for ultra-low-latency processing with millisecond latency but limited operator support.

options.text: `trigger(continuous='1 second')`
`gold_revenue` is a Streaming Live Table; it incrementally aggregates new rows appended to `silver_orders`
`gold_revenue` is a Materialized View (Live Table); it uses `dlt.read()` so it performs a full recomputation of the aggregation on each pipeline run
options.explanation: `dlt.read()` (not `dlt.read_stream()`) makes `gold_revenue` a materialized Live Table, also called a Materialized View in DLT terminology. Because it uses a static read (not streaming), DLT must recompute the full aggregation on every pipeline run — it cannot do incremental updates. This is appropriate for aggregations like revenue totals where correctness requires seeing all data. `dlt.read_stream()` would create a Streaming Live Table, which processes incrementally. Option **A** is wrong because `dlt.read()` (not `dlt.read_stream()`) was used. Option **C** is wrong because `@dlt.table` persists data (use `@dlt.view` for non-persisted views). Option **D** is wrong — `dlt.read()` on a streaming table reads the current materialized state, which is valid.

options.text: `gold_revenue` is a View; it does not persist data and is computed on every query
`gold_revenue` will fail at runtime because `dlt.read()` cannot be used on a Streaming Live Table

questions.stem:
An e-commerce company streams clickstream events to a Delta table. A downstream Structured Streaming job joins `clickstream` events with `product_catalog` (a static Delta table that updates daily). The data engineer uses the following pattern:

```python
clicks = spark.readStream.table('clickstream')
catalog = spark.read.table('product_catalog')  # static read
result = clicks.join(catalog, 'product_id')
```

What is a CRITICAL limitation of this approach?

options.text: Joining a stream with a static DataFrame is not supported in Structured Streaming
The `product_catalog` snapshot is loaded once at stream startup and will NOT reflect daily updates to the catalog without restarting the stream
options.explanation: Stream-to-static joins are supported in Structured Streaming, but the static DataFrame is evaluated (snapshotted) once at the start of the stream and held in memory for the lifetime of the stream. Daily updates to `product_catalog` will NOT be reflected in the join until the stream is restarted. This is a real operational limitation. Solutions: restart the stream daily, use a streaming join with `foreachBatch` and re-read the catalog inside the batch function, or use Delta Change Data Feed to stream catalog changes. Option **A** is incorrect — stream-static joins ARE supported. Option **C** is incorrect — watermarks are needed for stream-stream joins, not stream-static. option **D** may be a concern for large catalogs but is not the critical correctness issue described.

options.text: The join will fail because `product_id` must be a watermarked column when joining streams with static data
The static DataFrame is broadcast automatically, which will cause OOM errors on large catalogs

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to perform a CDC MERGE into a Silver Delta table using the following logic: update existing rows, insert new rows, and delete rows that no longer exist in the source. The source is a daily full snapshot. Which MERGE statement CORRECTLY implements this logic?

options.text: `MERGE INTO silver USING source ON silver.id = source.id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *`
`MERGE INTO silver USING source ON silver.id = source.id WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT * WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE`
options.explanation: When the source is a full daily snapshot, rows missing from the source should be deleted from the target. option **B** correctly handles all three cases: (1) `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET *` — row exists in both source and target: update it, (2) `WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *` — row is in source but not target: insert it, (3) `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE` — row is in target but not in source: delete it. This is the full-sync MERGE pattern. Option **A** is missing the delete clause. Option **C** is missing the UPDATE for matched rows. option **D** deletes matched rows instead of updating them — this would delete every row that exists in both tables.

options.text: `MERGE INTO silver USING source ON silver.id = source.id WHEN NOT MATCHED BY TARGET THEN INSERT * WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE THEN DELETE`
`MERGE INTO silver USING source ON silver.id = source.id WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *`

questions.stem: A data engineer is configuring Auto Loader with file notification mode on Azure. Select TWO statements that are TRUE about file notification mode compared to directory listing mode. (Select TWO)

options.text: File notification mode uses Azure Event Grid and Azure Queue Storage to receive push notifications when files are created, eliminating the need to list the entire directory
options.explanation: Option **A** is correct: on Azure, file notification mode integrates with Azure Event Grid (to detect new blobs) and Azure Queue Storage (as the message queue) — Auto Loader sets these up automatically with appropriate permissions, but the Azure resources are required. Option **C** is correct: the primary benefit of file notification mode is scale — for buckets with millions of files, listing the directory on every trigger is expensive (O(n) in total files); notification mode is event-driven and O(new files). Option **B** is wrong — it requires Azure Event Grid + Queue Storage to be provisioned. Option **D** is wrong — directory listing is the default. Option **E** is technically a concern but not a standard documented behavior of the service; the notification queue is generally reliable and Auto Loader does not rely solely on notifications (it has idempotency mechanisms).

options.text: File notification mode requires no additional Azure infrastructure and works out-of-the-box with any storage account
File notification mode scales better than directory listing mode for buckets with millions of files because it avoids repeated full directory scans
options.explanation: Option **A** is correct: on Azure, file notification mode integrates with Azure Event Grid (to detect new blobs) and Azure Queue Storage (as the message queue) — Auto Loader sets these up automatically with appropriate permissions, but the Azure resources are required. Option **C** is correct: the primary benefit of file notification mode is scale — for buckets with millions of files, listing the directory on every trigger is expensive (O(n) in total files); notification mode is event-driven and O(new files). Option **B** is wrong — it requires Azure Event Grid + Queue Storage to be provisioned. Option **D** is wrong — directory listing is the default. Option **E** is technically a concern but not a standard documented behavior of the service; the notification queue is generally reliable and Auto Loader does not rely solely on notifications (it has idempotency mechanisms).

options.text: File notification mode is the default mode when using `format('cloudFiles')`
File notification mode can miss files if the notification queue is purged before Auto Loader processes them

questions.stem: In a Delta Live Tables pipeline, a data engineer wants to track records that fail a data quality check WITHOUT stopping the pipeline and WITHOUT dropping the failed records. Which DLT expectation decorator achieves this?

options.text: `@dlt.expect_or_fail`
`@dlt.expect_or_drop`
`@dlt.expect`
options.explanation: `@dlt.expect` (or `@dlt.expect_all` for multiple constraints) records violations in the DLT event log and includes failed records in the output table — the pipeline continues and no data is dropped. This is the 'warn and continue' behavior. Compare: `@dlt.expect_or_drop` drops violating records and continues; `@dlt.expect_or_fail` halts the pipeline on any violation. `@dlt.expect` is ideal when you want visibility into data quality issues without interrupting the pipeline or losing data. The violation counts are surfaced in the DLT pipeline UI and event log. option **A** stops the pipeline. option **B** drops records. option **D** (`@dlt.expect_all`) applies multiple constraints but still uses the same 'warn' behavior — it is the correct multi-constraint version of `@dlt.expect`, but `@dlt.expect` is the specific single-constraint answer here.

options.text: `@dlt.expect_all`

questions.stem: A retail company implements a DLT pipeline using `APPLY CHANGES INTO` for CDC processing from a source that sends INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE events. The CDC source sometimes sends duplicate events with the same sequence number for the same key. How does `APPLY CHANGES INTO` handle these duplicates?

options.text: `APPLY CHANGES INTO` has no deduplication; all duplicates are written to the target table

`APPLY CHANGES INTO` deduplicates events based on the sequence column — only the event with the highest sequence number for each key is applied; ties are resolved by retaining the most recently received event
`APPLY CHANGES INTO` deduplicates based on the sequence column and ignores any duplicate event with the same key and sequence number
options.explanation: `APPLY CHANGES INTO` uses the `SEQUENCE BY` column to determine the correct ordering of CDC events and handles duplicates: if two events arrive with the same primary key AND the same sequence value, the second occurrence is treated as a duplicate and ignored. This is critical for exactly-once CDC semantics when source systems may replay events. The engine applies only the event with the highest sequence number per key. Option **A** is wrong — APPLY CHANGES INTO has built-in deduplication. Option **B** is partially correct about sequence-based ordering but wrong about 'ties' — duplicates (same key + same sequence) are idempotently dropped, not resolved by arrival order. Option **D** is wrong — duplicates do not cause failures.

options.text: `APPLY CHANGES INTO` fails and raises an error when duplicate sequence numbers are detected

questions.stem:
A data engineer reads CDF (Change Data Feed) data from a Delta table using:

```python
df = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .option('readChangeFeed', 'true') \
    .option('startingVersion', 5) \
    .table('inventory')
```

What additional column uniquely identifies whether a row represents a before-update value or an after-update value?

options.text: `_commit_version`
`_change_type`
options.explanation: The `_change_type` column in CDF output distinguishes the type of change for each row. For an UPDATE, two rows are emitted per changed row: one with `_change_type = 'update_preimage'` (the before-update values) and one with `_change_type = 'update_postimage'` (the after-update values). Other values include `'insert'` and `'delete'`. `_commit_version` tells you which transaction the change belongs to but doesn't distinguish pre/post-image. `_operation` and `_row_version` are not standard CDF columns. The full set of CDF metadata columns is: `_change_type`, `_commit_version`, and `_commit_timestamp`.

options.text: `_operation`
`_row_version`

questions.stem: In a Medallion architecture, a data engineer stores raw JSON event data exactly as received from a Kafka topic, without any transformation or schema enforcement. Which layer does this data belong to?

options.text: Gold layer
Silver layer
Bronze layer
options.explanation: The Bronze layer is the raw landing zone for data in a Medallion architecture. It stores data in its original format — no schema enforcement, no type casting, no deduplication. The goal is fidelity: capture everything exactly as it arrived. Storing raw Kafka JSON events directly into Bronze Delta tables is the canonical Bronze pattern (often using Auto Loader or a Kafka streaming connector). The Silver layer applies cleaning, typing, and deduplication. The Gold layer applies business aggregations. 'Staging' is not a standard Medallion layer name. option **A** (Gold) is aggregated/business-ready. option **B** (Silver) is cleansed and typed.

options.text: Staging layer

questions.stem: A data engineer attempts to write a DataFrame to an existing Delta table that has a different schema — the incoming DataFrame has an extra column `discount_pct` not present in the table. The write uses the default options. What happens?

options.text: The write succeeds; Delta silently ignores the extra column
The write fails with a schema mismatch error unless `mergeSchema` or `overwriteSchema` is enabled
options.explanation: By default, Delta Lake enforces schema on write — a write operation that presents columns not in the existing table schema will fail with an `AnalysisException` (schema mismatch). To allow new columns, you must either: (1) add `.option('mergeSchema', 'true')` to the write operation (adds the new column to the table schema), or (2) set `spark.databricks.delta.schema.autoMerge.enabled = true` at the session level. `overwriteSchema` is used when you want to replace the entire schema (usually with `mode('overwrite')`). Option **A** is wrong — Delta does not silently drop columns. option **C** describes the behavior when mergeSchema IS enabled. Option **D** is completely wrong — Delta tables accept new data by design.

options.text: The write succeeds and the `discount_pct` column is automatically added to the table schema
The write fails because Delta tables are immutable and cannot accept new data after creation

questions.stem: A data engineer implements SCD Type 2 using a `MERGE INTO` statement on a Delta table. The table has columns: `customer_id`, `email`, `is_current` (BOOLEAN), `effective_date`, `end_date`. When a customer updates their email, which MERGE clause pattern CORRECTLY implements the SCD2 logic?

options.text: `WHEN MATCHED AND target.is_current = true AND target.email != source.email THEN UPDATE SET is_current = false, end_date = current_date()`
options.explanation: SCD Type 2 with MERGE requires TWO operations: (1) Close the old record by setting `is_current = false` and `end_date = current_date()` — this is the MATCHED clause in option **A**. (2) Insert a new record with the updated email, `is_current = true`, `effective_date = current_date()`, `end_date = NULL` — this requires a separate INSERT (either in the same MERGE using `WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE` logic or via a subsequent INSERT). option **A** correctly implements step 1. Option **B** is SCD Type 1 (overwrites history by updating in place). option **C** deletes the record — loses history entirely. Option **D** is the new row INSERT step which is also needed, but alone without closing the old record it creates duplicate 'current' rows. The correct full MERGE has both A and D logic, but A best describes the critical SCD2-specific action.

options.text: `WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET email = source.email, effective_date = current_date()`
`WHEN MATCHED THEN DELETE`
`WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (customer_id, email, is_current, effective_date, end_date) VALUES (source.customer_id, source.email, true, current_date(), null)`

questions.stem: A data engineering team wants to enable Change Data Feed on an existing Delta table called `customer_profiles`. Which statement CORRECTLY enables CDF?

options.text: `ALTER TABLE customer_profiles SET TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true)`
options.explanation: Change Data Feed is enabled on an existing Delta table using `ALTER TABLE ... SET TBLPROPERTIES (delta.enableChangeDataFeed = true)`. The property key must be exactly `delta.enableChangeDataFeed` (with the `delta.` prefix). CDF only captures changes made AFTER it is enabled — historical data before enabling is not in the CDF. For new tables, you can set this in the CREATE TABLE statement with the same TBLPROPERTIES syntax. Option **B** is not valid SQL syntax for Databricks. option **C** uses CREATE TABLE (for existing tables you need ALTER TABLE) and omits the `delta.` prefix. Option **D** is not valid syntax — OPTIMIZE has nothing to do with enabling CDF.

options.text: `ALTER TABLE customer_profiles ENABLE CHANGE DATA FEED`
`CREATE TABLE customer_profiles TBLPROPERTIES (enableChangeDataFeed = true)`
`OPTIMIZE customer_profiles WITH CHANGE DATA FEED ENABLED`

questions.stem: A data engineer is deciding whether to partition a large Delta table by `country_code`. The table has 50 million rows, receives 10,000 new rows per day, and `country_code` has 200 distinct values. Select TWO factors that would make partitioning by `country_code` a GOOD choice. (Select TWO)

options.text: Most queries filter by `country_code`, enabling partition pruning to skip irrelevant data files
options.explanation: Option **A** is the primary benefit of partitioning: if queries commonly filter by `country_code`, Spark can skip entire partitions (directories) at the file-system level before even reading data — this is partition pruning, which is faster than data skipping because it avoids reading files entirely. Option **E** is important: partitioning is beneficial when partition sizes are large (ideally GB-scale). With 50M rows and growth over time, each of the 200 country partitions could accumulate hundreds of thousands of rows — making the partition sizes substantial enough to justify the overhead. Option **B** is actually a concern, not a benefit: 10,000 rows/day across 200 countries is only 50 rows per country per day — very small per-partition daily writes that would create tiny files. option **C** (200 values) is reasonable cardinality, but on its own doesn't justify partitioning. Option **D** is wrong — string is fine, but numeric types are also valid partition keys.

options.text: The table receives only 10,000 rows per day, which ensures each partition file is close to the 1GB target size
200 distinct values is a low-to-medium cardinality that creates a manageable number of partitions
`country_code` is a string column, which is required for Delta Lake partitioning
Each of the 200 country partitions will accumulate significant data over time, making file sizes large enough to benefit from partitioning
options.explanation: Option **A** is the primary benefit of partitioning: if queries commonly filter by `country_code`, Spark can skip entire partitions (directories) at the file-system level before even reading data — this is partition pruning, which is faster than data skipping because it avoids reading files entirely. Option **E** is important: partitioning is beneficial when partition sizes are large (ideally GB-scale). With 50M rows and growth over time, each of the 200 country partitions could accumulate hundreds of thousands of rows — making the partition sizes substantial enough to justify the overhead. Option **B** is actually a concern, not a benefit: 10,000 rows/day across 200 countries is only 50 rows per country per day — very small per-partition daily writes that would create tiny files. option **C** (200 values) is reasonable cardinality, but on its own doesn't justify partitioning. Option **D** is wrong — string is fine, but numeric types are also valid partition keys.

questions.stem:
A data engineer sets the following Delta table property:

```sql
ALTER TABLE events SET TBLPROPERTIES (
  'delta.logRetentionDuration' = 'interval 30 days'
);
```

What does this property control?

options.text: How long the Parquet data files are retained before VACUUM can delete them
How long the transaction log JSON files are retained, controlling the range of versions available for Time Travel
options.explanation: `delta.logRetentionDuration` controls how long Delta keeps the transaction log files (the JSON and checkpoint files in `_delta_log`). A longer retention means more historical versions are available for Time Travel queries. The default is 30 days. Importantly, this is distinct from `delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration`, which controls how long the actual Parquet data files are retained before VACUUM can clean them up. Both must be set appropriately to support Time Travel: the log must have the version entry AND the data files must still exist. option **A** describes `delta.deletedFileRetentionDuration`. option **C** describes Databricks caching (unrelated to table properties). option **D** describes Unity Catalog audit logging (a separate system).

options.text: How long the table data is cached in the Databricks cluster's memory
How long audit events for this table are stored in Unity Catalog

questions.stem: In Unity Catalog, what is the correct three-level namespace format for fully qualifying a table reference?

options.text: `workspace.database.table`
`catalog.schema.table`
options.explanation: Unity Catalog uses a three-level namespace: `catalog.schema.table`. The levels are: (1) Catalog — the top-level container, analogous to a database in other systems; (2) Schema — equivalent to a traditional database/schema, containing tables, views, and functions; (3) Table — the individual data object. Example: `main.sales.transactions`. This replaces the two-level namespace (`database.table`) used in the legacy Hive metastore. option **A** uses 'workspace' which is not a namespace level. option **C** uses 'database' instead of 'schema' — Unity Catalog uses 'schema'. option **D** uses 'metastore' which is the Unity Catalog infrastructure itself, not part of the namespace.

options.text: `catalog.database.table`
`metastore.catalog.table`

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to grant an external data science team access to read Parquet files stored in an Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 container, without granting them access to any other storage location. The organization uses Unity Catalog. Which sequence of steps is CORRECT?

options.text: Create an External Location referencing the container path and storage credential, then GRANT READ FILES on the External Location to the data science group
options.explanation: Unity Catalog external storage access has two components: (1) Storage Credential — wraps the cloud identity (Azure service principal or managed identity) that has cloud-level access to storage; (2) External Location — combines a Storage Credential with a specific cloud storage path (e.g., `abfss://container@account.dfs.core.windows.net/path`). To grant read-only access, you GRANT READ FILES (or WRITE FILES) ON EXTERNAL LOCATION to a principal. This scopes access to that specific path prefix, not all storage accessible by the credential. Option **B** is wrong — you can't GRANT READ FILES on a Storage Credential directly; you grant on the External Location. Option **C** is wrong — `USE CATALOG` is a Unity Catalog privilege for catalogs, not external locations. option **D** violates the principle of least privilege and bypasses Unity Catalog governance.

options.text: Create a Storage Credential pointing to the container path, then GRANT READ FILES on the Storage Credential
Create an External Location, then GRANT USE CATALOG on the External Location
Share the Azure service principal credentials directly with the data science team via dbutils.secrets

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to implement column masking so that users in the `analyst` group see only the last 4 digits of `credit_card_number`, while users in the `admin` group see the full value. Which approach CORRECTLY implements this in Unity Catalog?

options.text: Create a masking function that checks `is_member('admin')` and returns the full value for admins or a masked value for others, then apply it with `ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET MASK`
options.explanation: Unity Catalog's column masking feature allows you to attach a SQL function to a specific column. The masking function receives the column value and the calling user's identity, and returns the masked (or unmasked) value based on logic like `is_member('admin')`. The syntax is: `CREATE FUNCTION mask_card(value STRING) RETURN CASE WHEN is_member('admin') THEN value ELSE CONCAT('****-****-****-', RIGHT(value, 4)) END;` then `ALTER TABLE payments ALTER COLUMN credit_card_number SET MASK mask_card;`. All users query the same table with the same SQL, but the masking function transparently applies to each user based on their group membership. option **B** (two views) works but requires managing separate objects and access grants — the column masking approach is cleaner. Option **C** is not valid Unity Catalog syntax — there is no column-level GRANT SELECT with conditional data. Option **D** is a naming convention, not a security control.

options.text: Create two separate views — one for analysts with a masked column, one for admins with the full column — and grant each group access to their respective view
Use `GRANT SELECT ON COLUMN (credit_card_number) TO admin` to control column-level access
Add a `_masked` suffix to the column name and use a naming convention to signal which columns are sensitive

questions.stem: Unity Catalog automatically captures data lineage. Which of the following is TRUE about Unity Catalog's lineage tracking?

options.text: Lineage is only captured for tables created with the `LINEAGE ENABLED` option in the CREATE TABLE statement
Unity Catalog automatically captures table-level and column-level lineage for SQL queries and notebook operations without any configuration
options.explanation: Unity Catalog's lineage tracking is automatic and requires no configuration. It captures: (1) Table-level lineage — which upstream tables were read to produce a downstream table, (2) Column-level lineage — which source columns contributed to each target column, (3) Cross-table lineage across notebooks, jobs, DLT pipelines, and SQL queries. Lineage is visualized in the Catalog Explorer UI under each table. Option **A** is wrong — no special option is needed at table creation. Option **C** is wrong — lineage works for managed tables, external tables, and views. Option **D** is wrong — column-level lineage is explicitly one of the features, showing which columns were read and how they map to output columns.

options.text: Lineage tracking only works for Delta tables, not for external tables or views
Lineage is captured at the query level but cannot show which specific columns were read or derived

questions.stem: A data engineer uses `dbutils.secrets.get(scope='prod-secrets', key='db-password')` in a notebook. Select TWO statements that are TRUE about Databricks secret management. (Select TWO)

options.text: If the secret value is printed in a notebook cell output, Databricks automatically redacts it as `[REDACTED]`
options.explanation: Option **A** is correct: Databricks redacts secret values in notebook output — if you accidentally `print(dbutils.secrets.get(...))`, the output shows `[REDACTED]` instead of the actual value, preventing accidental credential exposure in notebook logs. Option **C** is correct: Databricks supports two types of secret scope backends — Databricks-backed (secrets stored in Databricks' encrypted store) and Azure Key Vault-backed (secrets live in Azure Key Vault; Databricks reads them via Key Vault access policies). Both are accessed with the same `dbutils.secrets.get()` API. Option **B** is wrong — two backends exist (Databricks + AKV). Option **D** is wrong — secret scopes are workspace-scoped; Unity Catalog does not have a cross-workspace secret sharing feature. Option **E** is partially correct (strings are returned) but Databricks recommends NOT embedding secrets directly in logs — the redaction only applies to cell output, not all string interpolation contexts.

options.text: Databricks-backed secret scopes store secrets in the Databricks control plane and are the only supported backend
Azure Key Vault-backed secret scopes allow secrets to be managed in Azure Key Vault while being accessed through the same `dbutils.secrets.get()` API
options.explanation: Option **A** is correct: Databricks redacts secret values in notebook output — if you accidentally `print(dbutils.secrets.get(...))`, the output shows `[REDACTED]` instead of the actual value, preventing accidental credential exposure in notebook logs. Option **C** is correct: Databricks supports two types of secret scope backends — Databricks-backed (secrets stored in Databricks' encrypted store) and Azure Key Vault-backed (secrets live in Azure Key Vault; Databricks reads them via Key Vault access policies). Both are accessed with the same `dbutils.secrets.get()` API. Option **B** is wrong — two backends exist (Databricks + AKV). Option **D** is wrong — secret scopes are workspace-scoped; Unity Catalog does not have a cross-workspace secret sharing feature. Option **E** is partially correct (strings are returned) but Databricks recommends NOT embedding secrets directly in logs — the redaction only applies to cell output, not all string interpolation contexts.

options.text: Secret scopes can be shared across workspaces by using Unity Catalog's cross-workspace secret sharing feature
Secrets retrieved with `dbutils.secrets.get()` are returned as Python strings and can be used directly in connection strings

questions.stem: A Spark job is running slow and a data engineer wants to understand the physical plan of a SQL query, including whether a broadcast join was used. Which Spark UI tab should they navigate to?

options.text: Executors tab
Environment tab
SQL / DataFrame tab
options.explanation: The SQL / DataFrame tab in the Spark UI shows the query execution plan for DataFrame and SQL operations. It displays the physical plan as a DAG of nodes, including join strategies (BroadcastHashJoin, SortMergeJoin, ShuffledHashJoin), exchange (shuffle) nodes, filter pushdowns, and scan operations. This is the correct place to verify whether AQE switched a sort-merge join to a broadcast join. The Executors tab shows resource usage per executor (tasks, memory, shuffle read/write). The Environment tab shows Spark configurations. The Storage tab shows cached RDD/DataFrame information.

options.text: Storage tab

questions.stem: A data engineer runs a Spark job that performs a large shuffle. After the job, they observe the following in the Spark UI Stage view for Stage 2: Shuffle Write: 50GB, Tasks: 200, Shuffle Spill (Disk): 30GB. What does the 30GB of disk spill indicate and what is the MOST likely fix?

options.text: 30GB of shuffle blocks were written to disk during the shuffle write phase due to network bandwidth limits; fix by increasing network bandwidth
30GB of in-memory shuffle data exceeded executor heap memory and was written to local disk; fix by increasing executor memory or reducing the number of rows per shuffle partition
options.explanation: Shuffle spill (disk) occurs when the data being buffered for a shuffle operation doesn't fit in executor memory (heap memory allocated to the shuffle buffer). Spark writes the overflow to local disk and reads it back later, causing a significant performance hit due to disk I/O being much slower than memory. The 30GB spill on a 50GB shuffle suggests executors are memory-constrained. Solutions: (1) Increase executor memory (`spark.executor.memory`), (2) Increase the number of shuffle partitions (`spark.sql.shuffle.partitions`) so each partition is smaller and fits in memory, (3) Increase executor cores (more memory per core). Option **A** is wrong — spill is a memory issue, not network bandwidth. option **C** describes data skew causing slow tasks, not disk spill. Option **D** is unrelated to shuffle spill.

options.text: 30GB of data was read from a remote executor due to data skew; fix by salting the join key
30GB of Delta table data was materialized temporarily during the OPTIMIZE operation

questions.stem: A Databricks job runs on an all-purpose cluster that is already running 3 other interactive notebooks. The job suddenly starts taking twice as long as usual. What is the MOST LIKELY reason related to cluster type?

options.text: All-purpose clusters are limited to 4 simultaneous users, so the job is queued
Resources on the all-purpose cluster are shared with the 3 running notebooks, reducing available cores and memory for the job
options.explanation: All-purpose clusters are shared resources — multiple notebooks and jobs running concurrently compete for the same pool of executor cores and memory. If 3 notebooks are actively running Spark jobs, they consume CPU and memory, leaving fewer resources for the scheduled job, which then takes longer to complete. This is a fundamental reason why production jobs should use job clusters (isolated, dedicated, spun up fresh for each run, terminated after) rather than all-purpose clusters. Job clusters avoid resource contention and also provide cost benefits (lower DBU rate). Option **A** is wrong — there is no hard user limit of 4 on all-purpose clusters. Option **C** is wrong — autoscaling is based on workload demand, not sharing. Option **D** is a cloud infrastructure issue unrelated to cluster type.

options.text: All-purpose clusters automatically scale down when shared, reducing node count
The job is running in a different availability zone from the cluster nodes, causing network latency

questions.stem: A data engineer enables Photon on a Databricks cluster and expects a significant speedup for a pipeline that heavily uses a Python UDF for text normalization. After enabling Photon, the performance is unchanged. What is the MOST LIKELY explanation?

options.text: Photon only accelerates SQL and DataFrame operations written in Python; the UDF is Python and not covered
Python UDFs execute in a separate Python process via inter-process serialization; Photon cannot accelerate this row-by-row Python execution, so the UDF remains the bottleneck
options.explanation: Photon is a vectorized query engine written in C++ that accelerates Delta Lake reads, SQL operations, and native Spark DataFrame operations. However, it explicitly does NOT accelerate Python UDFs. Python UDFs require row-by-row serialization between the JVM (where Photon runs) and a Python interpreter process — this serialization overhead is outside Photon's scope. The UDF execution bottleneck is unchanged because Photon hands off to Python for each row's UDF call. Solutions: replace the Python UDF with a Pandas/vectorized UDF (reduces serialization overhead via Arrow batches), or rewrite as a native SQL/built-in function. option **A** conflates Photon's Python API support with Python UDF acceleration — the Python API is fine, but the UDF execution itself isn't Photon-accelerated. Options **C** and **D** are wrong.

options.text: Photon requires the cluster to be restarted after enabling before it takes effect
Photon is only available on clusters with more than 10 worker nodes

questions.stem: When reviewing the Spark UI for a streaming job, a data engineer sees an 'Exchange' node in the query plan highlighted in orange/yellow. What does an Exchange node represent and why might it be a performance concern?

options.text: An Exchange node represents a file read operation; orange indicates the files are not cached
An Exchange node represents a shuffle operation where data must be redistributed across executors; it is a performance concern because shuffling requires network transfer and disk I/O
options.explanation: In the Spark UI SQL tab's query plan visualization, Exchange nodes represent shuffle operations — points in the plan where data must be redistributed across partitions (e.g., for GROUP BY, JOIN, or repartition). Shuffles are expensive because they require: (1) serializing data, (2) writing to disk (shuffle write), (3) transferring data across the network between executors, (4) reading from disk (shuffle read). An Exchange node highlighted in orange/yellow often indicates the shuffle is the performance bottleneck (it's showing high cost). Reducing shuffles (via broadcast joins, partition-aware joins, or pre-partitioned data) is a primary Spark optimization goal. option **A** describes file reads, which appear as 'Scan' nodes. option **C** describes broadcast joins, which appear as 'BroadcastExchange' but the orange highlight indicates cost, not a threshold warning. Option **D** is wrong.

options.text: An Exchange node represents a broadcast join; orange indicates the broadcast table exceeds the size threshold
An Exchange node represents a checkpoint write to cloud storage

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `VACUUM events RETAIN 168 HOURS`. What is the effect of this command?

options.text: Deletes all data files older than 168 hours regardless of whether they are referenced by the transaction log
Deletes unreferenced Parquet data files (files no longer part of the current table state) that are older than 168 hours
options.explanation: VACUUM physically deletes Parquet data files from cloud storage that meet TWO conditions: (1) they are NOT referenced by the current transaction log (i.e., they have been logically removed by an UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, or OPTIMIZE operation), AND (2) they are older than the specified retention period (168 hours = 7 days in this case, which is the default). VACUUM does NOT delete files currently referenced by the live table state, no matter how old they are. It also does NOT delete transaction log entries (that's handled by log retention settings). After VACUUM, Time Travel queries requesting versions that relied on the deleted files will fail. Option **A** is dangerously wrong — VACUUM never deletes files still referenced by the log. option **C** describes log compaction. Option **D** is wrong — VACUUM permanently deletes.

options.text: Deletes transaction log entries older than 168 hours to compact the Delta log
Marks files older than 168 hours as archived but does not physically delete them

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a shallow clone of a production Delta table for a data science team to use for experimentation:

```sql
CREATE TABLE dev.sandbox.experiments SHALLOW CLONE prod.main.transactions;
```

Two weeks later, the production team runs `VACUUM prod.main.transactions RETAIN 0 HOURS`. What happens to the shallow clone?

options.text: The shallow clone is unaffected because it has its own independent copy of data files
The shallow clone's queries will fail because VACUUM deleted the source data files that the clone references
options.explanation: A shallow clone copies only the Delta transaction log (metadata) and references the source table's Parquet data files in their original location — it does NOT copy the physical data files. When `VACUUM ... RETAIN 0 HOURS` is run on the source table, it deletes all unreferenced data files from the source storage location. Since the shallow clone's log points to these now-deleted files, any query on the shallow clone that needs to read data will fail with a FileNotFoundException. VACUUM with RETAIN 0 HOURS is especially dangerous — it removes even files that could support Time Travel. option **A** describes DEEP CLONE behavior. Option **C** is wrong — there is no automatic conversion. Option **D** is wrong — the clone's log references file paths, not copies of the files.

options.text: The shallow clone is automatically converted to a deep clone when the source files are vacuumed
The shallow clone retains its own transaction log copies of the deleted files and queries continue to work

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `OPTIMIZE` on a Delta table and then immediately queries it. Why might the query run FASTER after OPTIMIZE even for a query that doesn't benefit from ZORDER?

options.text: OPTIMIZE pre-caches all table data in executor memory, so queries read from cache instead of storage
OPTIMIZE compacts many small Parquet files into fewer large files, reducing the overhead of opening thousands of file handles and improving read throughput
options.explanation: Even without ZORDER, OPTIMIZE improves query performance by addressing the 'small files problem'. After many streaming micro-batches or frequent appends, a table can accumulate thousands of tiny Parquet files (sometimes KB-sized). Reading these requires: opening a file handle per file, reading file metadata (Parquet footer) per file, and making many small I/O requests. OPTIMIZE compacts these into larger files (~1GB target), so a query that previously opened 5,000 files now opens 10 files — dramatically reducing I/O overhead and improving throughput. This benefits ANY query type (scans, aggregations, joins). Option **A** is wrong — OPTIMIZE doesn't cache data. Option **C** is partially true (statistics are updated in the log) but not the primary speed benefit described. Option **D** is wrong — OPTIMIZE updates column statistics for data skipping, not AQE join selection.

options.text: OPTIMIZE rewrites the Delta log in a more compact format, reducing the time to parse the transaction log
OPTIMIZE applies column statistics that are used by AQE to automatically select faster join strategies

questions.stem: A data engineer applies `ZORDER BY (event_type, device_id)` to a Delta table. A query filters on `device_id` only (not `event_type`). Will the ZORDER benefit this query?

options.text: No — ZORDER only provides data skipping benefit for the first column listed; `device_id` as the second column gains no benefit
Yes — ZORDER co-locates data for all listed columns simultaneously using a Z-curve (space-filling curve), so filtering on `device_id` alone still reduces the number of files scanned
options.explanation: ZORDER uses a Z-curve (Morton code) space-filling curve to co-locate data with similar values across ALL specified columns simultaneously within each file. This means data with the same `device_id` is clustered together even though `event_type` was also in the ZORDER specification. A filter on `device_id` alone CAN skip files by reading the min/max statistics for `device_id` per file — files whose `device_id` range doesn't overlap the filter value are skipped. The multi-column ZORDER provides data skipping benefit for any subset of the specified columns, though the benefit is strongest when filtering on the ZORDER columns in order (similar to a compound index). Option **A** and **C** are wrong — ZORDER is not like a single-column index that only helps the first column. Option **D** is wrong — filters on any subset of ZORDER columns can benefit.

options.text: No — ZORDER provides data skipping only when ALL listed columns are included in the query filter
Yes — but only if the query also includes a filter on `event_type` to activate the ZORDER optimization

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to create an isolated copy of a production Delta table for disaster recovery testing. The copy must be fully independent so that VACUUM on the source or target does not affect the other, and the copy must include all historical versions for Time Travel. Select TWO statements that describe DEEP CLONE behavior that make it suitable for this use case. (Select TWO)

options.text: DEEP CLONE copies all Parquet data files to the destination location, making the copy fully independent of the source's storage
options.explanation: Option **A** is correct: DEEP CLONE physically copies all Parquet data files to the destination storage path — the clone has its own independent copies, so VACUUM, writes, or deletions on either table do not affect the other. Option **C** is correct: DEEP CLONE also copies the full Delta transaction log (`_delta_log`), preserving all commit history and enabling Time Travel queries on the clone just as on the source. Option **B** is true (DEEP CLONE IS incremental on re-runs), but 'incremental' is not why it's suitable for the stated use case — independence and history are. Option **D** is wrong — DEEP CLONE is a point-in-time snapshot operation, not a real-time sync. Option **E** is wrong — DEEP CLONE copies ALL data files referenced in the log, including those needed for historical versions.

options.text: DEEP CLONE is incremental — subsequent runs copy only files added since the last clone operation
DEEP CLONE copies the complete Delta transaction log, preserving all historical versions and enabling Time Travel on the clone
options.explanation: Option **A** is correct: DEEP CLONE physically copies all Parquet data files to the destination storage path — the clone has its own independent copies, so VACUUM, writes, or deletions on either table do not affect the other. Option **C** is correct: DEEP CLONE also copies the full Delta transaction log (`_delta_log`), preserving all commit history and enabling Time Travel queries on the clone just as on the source. Option **B** is true (DEEP CLONE IS incremental on re-runs), but 'incremental' is not why it's suitable for the stated use case — independence and history are. Option **D** is wrong — DEEP CLONE is a point-in-time snapshot operation, not a real-time sync. Option **E** is wrong — DEEP CLONE copies ALL data files referenced in the log, including those needed for historical versions.

options.text: DEEP CLONE runs synchronously in real-time, keeping the destination in continuous sync with the source
DEEP CLONE copies only the most recent version of data files, discarding historical versions to save storage

questions.stem: A data engineer reads CDF data from a Silver Delta table to feed a downstream Gold table. They use `startingVersion` to begin reading from a specific commit. Later, they want to read CDF changes BETWEEN two specific versions. Which options achieve this?

options.text: `.option('startingVersion', 10).option('endingVersion', 20)` to read only changes committed in versions 10 through 20
options.explanation: Delta Lake CDF batch reads support bounding the read range using `startingVersion` / `endingVersion` (by version number) OR `startingTimestamp` / `endingTimestamp` (by timestamp). Using `.option('startingVersion', 10).option('endingVersion', 20)` reads only the change records from commits 10 through 20, inclusive. This is efficient and avoids reading the full CDF history. The analogous timestamp syntax also works. Option **B** is functionally correct but inefficient — it reads all CDF data from version 10 onward (potentially huge) and then filters in memory, whereas option **A** pushes the range filter to the reader. option **C** uses timestamps rather than versions (which is valid but not what the question asks). Option **D** is wrong — batch CDF reads fully support version ranges.

options.text: `.option('startingVersion', 10)` and then filter the result by `_commit_version <= 20` after reading
`.option('startingTimestamp', '2024-01-10').option('endingTimestamp', '2024-01-20')` to read by date range, then filter by version
Only streaming CDF reads support version ranges; batch CDF reads always start from version 0

questions.stem: A data engineer builds a DLT pipeline that processes orders from multiple countries. They use `APPLY CHANGES INTO` with `KEYS (order_id)` and `SEQUENCE BY event_timestamp`. A new requirement says that for deleted orders, the record should be KEPT in the table but marked with `is_deleted = true` rather than physically removed. Which `APPLY CHANGES INTO` clause supports this?

options.text: Add `IGNORE DELETE` to suppress all delete events and handle them manually
Add `APPLY AS DELETE WHEN operation_type = 'DELETE'` to route deletes to a separate quarantine table
Add `APPLY AS TRUNCATE WHEN ...` to mark rows as soft-deleted in-place
Add `STORED AS SCD TYPE 2` to preserve historical state including logical deletes
options.explanation: `APPLY CHANGES INTO` supports SCD Type 2 with the `STORED AS SCD TYPE 2` clause. In SCD2 mode, instead of physically updating or deleting rows, the pipeline: (1) Closes the old row by updating its `__END_AT` column (or a custom column), (2) Inserts a new row for every change (including deletes), preserving the full change history. Delete events result in a new row marked with the deleted state rather than physically removing the row. This is exactly what 'soft delete' (keep the record, mark it deleted) requires. option **A** (`IGNORE DELETE`) simply ignores DELETE events in the CDC stream, never applying them to the target — the record stays as-is with no deletion marker. Option **B** is not a valid `APPLY CHANGES INTO` clause. option **C** (`APPLY AS TRUNCATE`) is not a real APPLY CHANGES clause.

questions.stem: A data engineer is setting up a Structured Streaming job that reads from Kafka. They want to start from the very beginning of the Kafka topic's retention window on the first run. Which `startingOffsets` value achieves this?

options.text: `'latest'` — reads only messages produced after the stream starts
`'earliest'` — reads all messages available in the Kafka topic from the beginning of retention
options.explanation: `startingOffsets = 'earliest'` instructs the Structured Streaming Kafka source to start reading from the earliest available offset in all topic partitions when the stream first starts (no checkpoint exists). This is the correct value to consume the entire Kafka retention window on the first run. Remember: after the first run, the checkpoint takes over and `startingOffsets` is ignored for subsequent restarts. The alternative `'latest'` starts reading only new messages produced after the stream begins — it skips all historical data. option **C** (`'beginning'`) is not a valid Kafka source option in Spark. option **D** (`'reset'`) is not a valid Spark Streaming option.

options.text: `'beginning'` — reads from offset 0 in all partitions
`'reset'` — resets the consumer group offsets to the beginning

questions.stem: A data engineering team is considering using `mapGroupsWithState` vs `flatMapGroupsWithState` for a custom stateful aggregation. Select TWO TRUE statements that differentiate the two APIs. (Select TWO)

options.text: `mapGroupsWithState` emits exactly one output row per key per trigger; `flatMapGroupsWithState` can emit zero, one, or multiple output rows per key per trigger
options.explanation: Option **A** is the key functional difference: `mapGroupsWithState` is a 1-to-1 mapping — for each key group in a trigger, it MUST emit exactly one output row. `flatMapGroupsWithState` is 1-to-many (or 0) — it can emit any number of rows (including none) per key per trigger. This makes `flatMapGroupsWithState` more flexible for scenarios like session windowing (emit nothing during session, emit summary when session closes). Option **C** is correct: both APIs support state timeouts — `GroupStateTimeout.ProcessingTimeTimeout` (evict after N minutes of processing time) and `GroupStateTimeout.EventTimeTimeout` (evict based on event watermark). Option **B** is wrong — `flatMapGroupsWithState` supports update and append modes, not complete; `mapGroupsWithState` supports update mode. Option **D** is wrong — both APIs allow the output type to differ from the state type. Option **E** is wrong — `flatMapGroupsWithState` is NOT deprecated.

options.text: `flatMapGroupsWithState` supports both append and update output modes; `mapGroupsWithState` only supports complete mode
Both `mapGroupsWithState` and `flatMapGroupsWithState` support state timeouts for evicting idle state keys
options.explanation: Option **A** is the key functional difference: `mapGroupsWithState` is a 1-to-1 mapping — for each key group in a trigger, it MUST emit exactly one output row. `flatMapGroupsWithState` is 1-to-many (or 0) — it can emit any number of rows (including none) per key per trigger. This makes `flatMapGroupsWithState` more flexible for scenarios like session windowing (emit nothing during session, emit summary when session closes). Option **C** is correct: both APIs support state timeouts — `GroupStateTimeout.ProcessingTimeTimeout` (evict after N minutes of processing time) and `GroupStateTimeout.EventTimeTimeout` (evict based on event watermark). Option **B** is wrong — `flatMapGroupsWithState` supports update and append modes, not complete; `mapGroupsWithState` supports update mode. Option **D** is wrong — both APIs allow the output type to differ from the state type. Option **E** is wrong — `flatMapGroupsWithState` is NOT deprecated.

options.text: `mapGroupsWithState` requires the output type to match the state type; `flatMapGroupsWithState` allows a different output type
`flatMapGroupsWithState` is deprecated in Spark 3.x and replaced by `mapGroupsWithState`

questions.stem: A data engineer configures an Auto Loader pipeline with `cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode = 'addNewColumns'`. A new file arrives with a column `promo_code` not in the existing schema. What happens?

options.text: The pipeline fails with a schema mismatch error and stops until the schema is manually updated
The `promo_code` column is automatically added to the target table schema and the new file is processed; existing rows have NULL for `promo_code`
options.explanation: `cloudFiles.schemaEvolutionMode = 'addNewColumns'` (which is the default for Auto Loader when `mergeSchema` is enabled in the target write) tells Auto Loader to automatically evolve the schema when new columns appear in incoming files. The new `promo_code` column is added to the persisted schema in `schemaLocation` and the target table schema is updated. Existing rows that were processed before `promo_code` appeared will have NULL for that column. The pipeline continues without interruption. This is one of Auto Loader's key advantages over COPY INTO — seamless schema evolution without manual intervention. option **A** describes the behavior when schema evolution is disabled. option **C** (silent drop) is the `'rescue'` behavior for unknown columns when rescue column isn't set. Option **D** is not standard Auto Loader behavior.

options.text: The `promo_code` column is silently dropped from the incoming data and the file is processed with only the known columns
The entire file is routed to a schema mismatch quarantine table

questions.stem: A data engineer uses `foreachBatch` in a Structured Streaming pipeline to write to multiple Delta tables from a single stream in one micro-batch. What is the PRIMARY reason to use `foreachBatch` for multi-table writes instead of writing separate streams?

options.text: `foreachBatch` processes the micro-batch DataFrame only once, allowing multiple writes (to different tables, formats, or targets) without re-reading from the source
options.explanation: `foreachBatch` provides the streaming DataFrame as a batch DataFrame to a user-defined function. Inside that function, you can call `.write` multiple times to write to different tables, formats, or external systems — all using the SAME micro-batch data without re-reading the source. Without `foreachBatch`, to write to two Delta tables you would need two separate streaming queries, each independently reading from the source (doubling the source read cost). With `foreachBatch`, the micro-batch is materialized once and reused. This is especially valuable for fan-out pipelines (one stream feeding multiple sinks) and for combining streaming with non-streaming operations (e.g., MERGE INTO inside the batch function). Option **B** is wrong — no cross-cluster distribution. Option **C** is wrong — you can write to Delta without `foreachBatch`. Option **D** is wrong — checkpoints are still used in `foreachBatch` mode.

options.text: `foreachBatch` automatically distributes the micro-batch across multiple clusters for parallel writing
`foreachBatch` is the only way to write streaming data to Delta tables
`foreachBatch` converts the streaming write to a batch write, removing the need for checkpoints

questions.stem:
A data engineer wants to add a deletion vector-compatible property to a Delta table to improve MERGE and DELETE performance. They set:

```sql
ALTER TABLE orders SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.enableDeletionVectors' = 'true');
```

How do deletion vectors change the behavior of DELETE and MERGE operations?

options.text: Deletion vectors cause DELETE to immediately physically remove rows from Parquet files, eliminating the need for VACUUM
Deletion vectors store a bitmap of deleted rows separately from the data files, so DELETE and MERGE can mark rows as deleted without rewriting entire Parquet files
options.explanation: Deletion vectors (DV) are a Delta Lake feature that changes how DELETE and MERGE physically handle row removals. Without DVs, deleting a row from a Parquet file requires rewriting the entire Parquet file (excluding the deleted row) — expensive for large files with few deletes. With DVs enabled, a small sidecar file (a bitmap) records which row offsets within a Parquet file have been logically deleted, WITHOUT rewriting the Parquet file. Reads skip the DV-marked rows transparently. This dramatically speeds up DELETE and MERGE operations, especially when only a small fraction of rows in a file are affected. DVs are particularly valuable for GDPR/CCPA 'right to be forgotten' use cases with frequent targeted deletes. Eventually, OPTIMIZE can consolidate by physically rewriting files without the deleted rows. Option **A** is wrong — DVs defer the physical removal. Options **C** and **D** are fabricated behaviors.

options.text: Deletion vectors replicate deleted rows to a separate `_deleted` Delta table for audit purposes
Deletion vectors enable parallel MERGE operations by splitting the target table into shards

questions.stem:
A data engineer creates a generated column in a Delta table partitioned by that generated column:

```sql
CREATE TABLE events (
  event_id BIGINT,
  event_ts TIMESTAMP,
  event_month STRING GENERATED ALWAYS AS (DATE_FORMAT(event_ts, 'yyyy-MM'))
) PARTITIONED BY (event_month);
```

A query filters by `WHERE event_ts >= '2024-06-01' AND event_ts < '2024-07-01'`. What optimization does Delta apply?

options.text: No optimization; queries must filter on `event_month` directly to benefit from partitioning
Delta infers that `event_ts` in June maps to `event_month = '2024-06'` and applies partition pruning, skipping all non-June partitions
options.explanation: Generated column partition pruning is a key Delta Lake optimization. When a table is partitioned by a generated column whose expression is a deterministic function of another column, Delta's optimizer can infer partition values from filters on the source column. A filter `WHERE event_ts >= '2024-06-01' AND event_ts < '2024-07-01'` maps precisely to `event_month = '2024-06'`. Delta automatically applies this partition pruning, eliminating the need to scan partitions for all other months. This makes the partitioning transparent to query writers — they filter on the natural timestamp column without needing to know about the generated partitioning column. Option **A** is wrong — the optimization IS applied automatically. Option **C** is technically what happens internally but 'rewriting the query' is not the precise description. Option **D** is not related to generated columns.

options.text: Delta rewrites the query to replace `event_ts` filters with `event_month` filters automatically
Delta caches the June partition in memory for future queries

questions.stem:
A data engineer runs the following on a Delta table:

```sql
ALTER TABLE products ADD CONSTRAINT price_positive CHECK (price > 0);
```

There are currently 5 rows in the table with `price = 0` (legacy data). What happens?

options.text: The constraint is added successfully and the 5 rows with `price = 0` are automatically deleted
The command fails because existing rows violate the constraint; existing data must be cleaned before the constraint can be added
The constraint is added successfully; existing data is NOT checked, only future writes are validated
options.explanation: Delta Lake's `ALTER TABLE ADD CONSTRAINT CHECK` adds the constraint metadata to the transaction log WITHOUT retroactively validating existing rows. Existing data is grandfathered in — the 5 rows with `price = 0` remain in the table unchanged. Only subsequent INSERT, UPDATE, and MERGE operations are validated against the constraint. If a future write attempts to insert a row with `price = 0` or `price < 0`, it will fail. This behavior is different from some relational databases that validate existing data when adding a constraint. It is important to clean existing violating data before adding a constraint if you want full data integrity guarantees. Option **A** is wrong — no rows are deleted. Option **B** is wrong — Delta does not retroactively validate. Option **D** is wrong — no marker column is added.

options.text: The constraint is added but the 5 violating rows are flagged with a `_constraint_violated` marker column

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to rename a column in a Delta table from `user_name` to `username` without rewriting data files. Which prerequisite must be met before a column can be renamed in Delta Lake?

options.text: The table must be partitioned by the column being renamed
Column mapping must be enabled on the table (`delta.columnMapping.mode = 'name'`)
options.explanation: Column rename (and `DROP COLUMN`) in Delta Lake require column mapping to be enabled. Column mapping (`delta.columnMapping.mode = 'name'`) decouples the logical column name (what users see in SQL) from the physical field name in Parquet files. Without column mapping, the logical name IS the physical name — renaming would require rewriting all Parquet files. With column mapping, only the transaction log metadata is updated (mapping `username` → the internal physical field ID), and all existing Parquet files remain unchanged. To enable: `ALTER TABLE t SET TBLPROPERTIES ('delta.columnMapping.mode' = 'name', 'delta.minReaderVersion' = '2', 'delta.minWriterVersion' = '5')`. Then: `ALTER TABLE t RENAME COLUMN user_name TO username`. Options A, C, and **D** are not prerequisites for column renaming.

options.text: The column must have no data skipping statistics collected for it
The table must have fewer than 1,000 data files

questions.stem: An organization uses Delta Sharing to share a table with an external partner. The partner needs to read only rows from the shared table where `region = 'EMEA'`. Which Delta Sharing feature supports row-level filtering of shared data?

options.text: Create a share containing a view that filters the table by `region = 'EMEA'` instead of sharing the base table directly
options.explanation: Delta Sharing allows sharing Delta tables and views. To share only a subset of rows with a partner, the best practice is to create a view that filters the underlying table and then add the view to the share instead of the base table. Example: `CREATE VIEW prod.sales.emea_summary AS SELECT * FROM prod.sales.orders WHERE region = 'EMEA'; ALTER SHARE partner_share ADD TABLE prod.sales.emea_summary;`. The partner receives only the EMEA-filtered data when they query the shared table. Option **B** is not valid Delta Sharing syntax — `ALTER SHARE` does not accept WHERE clauses. Option **C** is not valid syntax for Delta Sharing GRANTs. option **D** (`CREATE SHARING POLICY`) is not a Delta Sharing feature.

options.text: Add `WHERE region = 'EMEA'` to the `ALTER SHARE` statement
Grant the recipient `SELECT WHERE region = 'EMEA'` access on the table
Enable Delta Sharing row-level security via `CREATE SHARING POLICY`

questions.stem: A company grants a junior data engineer `CREATE TABLE` privilege on a schema in Unity Catalog. The engineer creates a new Delta table in that schema. Who is the OWNER of the newly created table by default, and what implications does this have?

options.text: The schema owner automatically becomes the table owner; the creator has no implicit ownership
The data engineer who created the table is the table owner; they can ALTER, DROP, and GRANT privileges on the table to other principals
options.explanation: In Unity Catalog, the principal who creates an object (table, view, schema, etc.) becomes its OWNER by default. Object ownership grants full control: the owner can ALTER the table, DROP the table, and GRANT or REVOKE privileges on the table to other users. Ownership can be transferred with `ALTER TABLE SET OWNER TO <principal>`. This has governance implications: if a data engineer who owns critical tables leaves the organization, their tables' ownership should be transferred to a group or service principal. Option **A** is wrong — schema ownership doesn't transfer to table objects created within it. Option **C** is wrong — admins don't auto-own objects. Option **D** is wrong — ownership is assigned at creation time to the creator.

options.text: The Databricks workspace admin is always the default owner of all Unity Catalog objects
Tables created in Unity Catalog have no owner until one is explicitly assigned with `ALTER TABLE SET OWNER`

questions.stem: A data engineer wants to list all tags applied to tables in the `prod.finance` schema to discover which tables contain sensitive financial data. Which SQL query returns this information?

options.text: `SELECT * FROM prod.finance.information_schema.table_tags WHERE catalog_name = 'prod' AND schema_name = 'finance'`
options.explanation: Unity Catalog's `INFORMATION_SCHEMA` within each catalog contains views for metadata. The `table_tags` view shows tags applied to tables: `SELECT * FROM prod.information_schema.table_tags WHERE schema_name = 'finance'` returns all table tags in the `prod.finance` schema including the tag key-value pairs. Similarly, `column_tags` exists for column-level tags. This enables programmatic discovery of tagged objects for governance workflows. option **B** (`SHOW TAGS ON SCHEMA`) shows tags applied TO the schema object itself, not tags on tables within the schema. option **C** queries the audit log, not tag metadata. option **D** references a non-existent `delta.table_tags` table.

options.text: `SHOW TAGS ON SCHEMA prod.finance`
`SELECT * FROM system.access.audit WHERE tags IS NOT NULL AND schema_name = 'finance'`
`SELECT * FROM delta.table_tags WHERE schema = 'prod.finance'`

questions.stem: A data engineer is reviewing Unity Catalog's metastore-level vs catalog-level vs schema-level privileges. Select TWO statements that CORRECTLY describe privilege scope and inheritance. (Select TWO)

options.text: A privilege granted at the catalog level (e.g., `SELECT ON CATALOG`) automatically applies to all schemas and tables within that catalog
`USE CATALOG` must be granted at the catalog level; it cannot be inherited from a higher level
Granting `CREATE SCHEMA` at the catalog level allows the principal to create new schemas within that specific catalog
options.explanation: Option **C** is correct: `GRANT CREATE SCHEMA ON CATALOG prod TO engineer` grants the ability to create new schemas specifically within the `prod` catalog — this is a catalog-scoped privilege that enables DDL at the schema level. Option **D** is correct: `GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA prod.sales TO analyst` grants SELECT on ALL tables and views currently in (and future ones added to) the `prod.sales` schema — privileges at the schema level DO propagate to child objects (tables, views). Option **A** is wrong — Unity Catalog does NOT have `SELECT ON CATALOG` as an inheritable privilege that flows to tables; `USE CATALOG` enables navigation, but SELECT must be granted at the schema or table level. Option **B** is wrong — `USE CATALOG` is a catalog-level privilege, but the statement 'cannot be inherited from a higher level' is confusing because there is no higher level than catalog for this privilege. Option **E** is wrong — `CREATE CATALOG` is a metastore-level privilege but does not auto-grant access to existing catalogs.

options.text: A privilege granted on a schema (e.g., `SELECT ON SCHEMA`) automatically applies to all tables and views within that schema
options.explanation: Option **C** is correct: `GRANT CREATE SCHEMA ON CATALOG prod TO engineer` grants the ability to create new schemas specifically within the `prod` catalog — this is a catalog-scoped privilege that enables DDL at the schema level. Option **D** is correct: `GRANT SELECT ON SCHEMA prod.sales TO analyst` grants SELECT on ALL tables and views currently in (and future ones added to) the `prod.sales` schema — privileges at the schema level DO propagate to child objects (tables, views). Option **A** is wrong — Unity Catalog does NOT have `SELECT ON CATALOG` as an inheritable privilege that flows to tables; `USE CATALOG` enables navigation, but SELECT must be granted at the schema or table level. Option **B** is wrong — `USE CATALOG` is a catalog-level privilege, but the statement 'cannot be inherited from a higher level' is confusing because there is no higher level than catalog for this privilege. Option **E** is wrong — `CREATE CATALOG` is a metastore-level privilege but does not auto-grant access to existing catalogs.

options.text: Metastore-level privileges like `CREATE CATALOG` are inherited by all catalogs in the metastore automatically

questions.stem: A data engineer needs to understand whether a slow query is limited by CPU, memory, or I/O. They want to see cluster-level resource utilization (CPU %, memory %, network I/O) during the job run — not just Spark task metrics. Which Databricks tool provides this?

options.text: The Spark UI Stages tab — it shows per-executor CPU and memory utilization
The Ganglia metrics UI (accessible via the cluster page) — it shows historical cluster-wide CPU, memory, network, and disk I/O metrics
options.explanation: Ganglia is a cluster monitoring tool integrated into Databricks that shows OS-level metrics: CPU utilization (per core and aggregate), memory usage, network I/O (bytes sent/received), and disk I/O — for each node in the cluster over time. It is accessible from the cluster detail page under the 'Ganglia Metrics' tab. Ganglia is the right tool when you suspect resource-level bottlenecks (e.g., all CPUs pegged at 100% → compute-bound, high memory usage → memory pressure, high network I/O → shuffle-heavy). The Spark UI shows task-level Spark metrics (rows processed, shuffle sizes, duration) but not OS-level resource utilization. Option **A** is wrong — Spark UI shows task metrics, not CPU/memory utilization. option **C** shows query plan cost estimates, not runtime resource usage. Option **D** is not a real Databricks API.

options.text: The Spark UI SQL tab — it shows query-level CPU cost in milliseconds
`dbutils.cluster.getMetrics()` — it returns real-time CPU and memory metrics for the active cluster

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `ANALYZE TABLE prod.sales.orders COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS (order_date, customer_id)`. When does the cost-based optimizer ACTUALLY use these statistics?

options.text: Immediately on the next query, regardless of query structure
Only when the query involves multi-table JOINs or subqueries where cardinality estimation matters for join ordering and strategy selection, and `spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true`
options.explanation: The cost-based optimizer (CBO) uses `ANALYZE TABLE` statistics to estimate cardinalities and join selectivities for complex queries — particularly those involving multi-table JOINs (to determine optimal join ordering and type) and subqueries with filters. The CBO is enabled in Databricks by default (`spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true`), but it only makes a difference for queries where the optimizer has choices to make (e.g., should table A join table B first, or table A join table C first?). For simple single-table scans or queries with obvious structures, the CBO statistics don't change the plan. Delta Lake's file-level min/max statistics (for data skipping) are separate from CBO statistics — both exist simultaneously. option **A** overstates the impact. Option **C** is wrong — CBO works for batch, not streaming. Option **D** is wrong — CBO is enabled by default in Databricks.

options.text: Only for streaming queries; batch queries use file-level statistics from the Delta transaction log
The statistics are stored but the CBO is disabled in Databricks by default and must be explicitly turned on per query

questions.stem: A Structured Streaming job has been running for 6 hours. A data engineer checks the streaming query progress using `query.lastProgress` in Python. Which metric tells them how many rows per second are currently being processed?

options.text: `query.lastProgress['numInputRows']`
`query.lastProgress['processedRowsPerSecond']`
options.explanation: `processedRowsPerSecond` in the streaming query progress report is the metric for the processing throughput rate — it measures how many rows per second the streaming engine processed in the most recent micro-batch (calculated as `numInputRows / triggerExecution latency in seconds`). The other options: `numInputRows` is the absolute count of rows in the last micro-batch (not a rate), `latencyMs['triggerExecution']` is the total time in ms for the last micro-batch, and `stateOperators[0]['numRowsTotal']` is the total number of keys in the stateful operator's state store. For monitoring throughput and whether the stream is keeping up with input, `processedRowsPerSecond` vs `inputRowsPerSecond` is the key comparison.

options.text: `query.lastProgress['latencyMs']['triggerExecution']`
`query.lastProgress['stateOperators'][0]['numRowsTotal']`

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `DESCRIBE HISTORY prod.sales.orders` and sees an entry with `operation = 'MERGE'` and `operationParameters` containing `{'predicate': '["(order_id#123 = order_id#456)"]', 'matchedPredicates': ..., 'notMatchedPredicates': ...}`. What additional metric in `operationMetrics` would help them understand how many rows were actually modified?

options.text: `numFiles` — the number of Parquet files written during the MERGE
`numTargetRowsUpdated` and `numTargetRowsInserted` — the counts of rows updated and inserted by the MERGE
options.explanation: `DESCRIBE HISTORY` includes an `operationMetrics` map for each operation. For MERGE operations, the key metrics include: `numTargetRowsUpdated` (rows matched and updated), `numTargetRowsInserted` (rows from source with no match, inserted), `numTargetRowsDeleted` (rows deleted by WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE or WHEN MATCHED DELETE), `numSourceRows` (total rows read from source), and `numOutputRows` (total rows in the output). These metrics allow the engineer to verify that the MERGE had the expected impact — e.g., if `numTargetRowsUpdated = 0` when updates were expected, there may be a predicate issue. option **A** (`numFiles`) shows write volume but not row-level impact. Option **C** and **D** are available but don't show row-level change counts.

options.text: `numOutputBytes` — the total bytes written by the MERGE operation
`executionTimeMs` — the wall-clock time taken to execute the MERGE

questions.stem: A data engineer runs `CONVERT TO DELTA` on a partitioned Parquet table at `s3://bucket/events/` partitioned by `(year INT, month INT)`. Which is the CORRECT syntax?

options.text: ``CONVERT TO DELTA parquet.`s3://bucket/events/` ``
options.explanation: It omits `PARTITIONED BY`, so partition metadata would not be registered.

options.text: ``CONVERT TO DELTA parquet.`s3://bucket/events/` PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)``
options.explanation: is correct. The full syntax to convert a partitioned Parquet table is:

options.text: `ALTER TABLE s3://bucket/events/ CONVERT FORMAT TO DELTA PARTITIONED BY (year INT, month INT)`
options.explanation: `ALTER TABLE ... CONVERT FORMAT TO DELTA` is not valid Databricks SQL syntax.

options.text: `CREATE TABLE events USING DELTA LOCATION 's3://bucket/events/' PARTITIONED BY (year, month)`
options.explanation: `CREATE TABLE ... USING DELTA LOCATION ...` creates a new Delta table pointing at existing files but does not convert them; it would fail if the files are Parquet, not Delta.

questions.stem: A data engineer creates a Bloom filter index on a column with only 3 distinct values (`'low'`, `'medium'`, `'high'`). Is this an effective use of a Bloom filter index?

options.text: Yes — Bloom filters work for any cardinality and will effectively skip files that don't contain the target value
No — for low-cardinality columns with few distinct values, each file likely contains all values, so the Bloom filter will rarely result in file skipping; ZORDER or partitioning is more appropriate
options.explanation: Bloom filter indexes are most effective for HIGH-cardinality columns where individual values are rare in any given file. For a column with only 3 distinct values (`'low'`, `'medium'`, `'high'`), any reasonably large file will contain all three values. A Bloom filter for `value = 'low'` would return 'possibly present' for essentially EVERY file (since most files do contain at least some 'low' rows) — resulting in no file skipping whatsoever. The filter provides no benefit. Better alternatives: (1) Partition by the low-cardinality column (3 distinct values = 3 manageable partitions), or (2) ZORDER by the column to co-locate matching rows within files, enabling min/max statistics-based skipping. Option **A** is wrong — effectiveness depends heavily on cardinality. Option **C** is incorrect about the false positive rate relationship to cardinality. Option **D** is wrong — Bloom filters support string columns.

options.text: Yes — Bloom filters for low-cardinality columns have a lower false positive rate than for high-cardinality columns
No — Bloom filters only support numeric columns; string columns like `'low'`, `'medium'`, `'high'` are not supported

questions.stem:
A data engineer writes to a Delta table partitioned by `region` using:

```python
spark.conf.set('spark.sql.sources.partitionOverwriteMode', 'dynamic')
```
